summary: Jeff Atwood argues that when choosing between IE7 and Firefox, the community surrounding a product matters more than the product itself. While both browsers are comparable as modern web browsers, Firefox's vibrant ecosystem of extensions and add-ons gives it a decisive advantage. Microsoft's failure to cultivate community—requiring C programming for add-ons, outsourcing their extension site, and lacking cultural enthusiasm—explains IE's weakness. However, Atwood acknowledges that popularity brings risks, as the same extensibility that enables Firefox's thriving community also enabled IE's spyware problem.
key_points: Firefox and IE7 are roughly equivalent products technically, but Firefox wins because of its stronger community and extension ecosystem
Microsoft's technical decisions (requiring C for add-ons) and cultural indifference have prevented an IE community from forming
The same extensibility features that enable helpful add-ons can also enable malware—the difference is who your community attracts
Community doesn't happen accidentally; it must be intentionally cultivated through accessible tooling and active engagement
Open source culture creates fervor and devotion that proprietary products struggle to replicate
money_quotes: I visit websites, which I consider more important than what I use to view said websites.
It's not that Firefox is inherently a better product than IE7. It isn't. But Firefox has a stronger, more vibrant user community, and that ultimately makes it a better product than IE7.
Is the community around a product more important than the product itself?
There are far more people in the world capable of writing scripts than are capable of writing C code.
Community support can make or break a product.

The big difference is that Firefox has a community of web developers and hobbyists who build cool applications for it while most of the folks extending Internet Explorer in the Windows world are writing spyware and other kinds of malware.
key_insight: A product's community ecosystem often matters more than the product's inherent quality, making community cultivation a critical but often neglected competitive advantage.
